The average rate of change is a concept in mathematics that measures the average change in the value of a function, between two points. nnIn other words, it tells you how much the function\'s output (y-value) changes per unit change in the input (x-value), on average, between two specific input values.nnIn more mathematical terms, if you have a function $f(x)$ and you want to find the average rate of change between the points $a$ and $b$, you would calculate it as: nn$$nfrac{{f(b) - f(a)}}{{b - a}}n$$ nnThis formula gives you the slope of the line that connects the points $(a, f(a))$ and $(b, f(b))$ on the graph of the function - also known as secant line. nnFor example, if you wanted to find the average rate of change of the function $f(x)=x^2$ from $x=2$ to $x=4$, you would substitute the x-values into the formula and get nn$$nfrac{{f(4) - f(2)}}{{4 - 2}} = frac{{16 - 4}}{2} = 6n$$ nnThis means that, on average, the function $f(x)=x^2$ changes by 6 units for every unit change in $x$ in the interval from $x=2$ to $x=4$.
